More capital actually constrains his choices—investors expect revenue acceleration, not a pause to build internal R&D. This answer inverts the real trade-off: traction doesn't give him more freedom; it gives him less.

questions.question_text: What happens to his competitive advantage if he waits two months and a funded competitor launches in the meantime?

options.option_text: It collapses. The competitor enters with capital, scales to dozens of customers, generates the data leverage that Carlos was trying to build, and then uses that advantage to out-iterate him. Carlos's two-month delay hands them the very moat he was trying to secure for himself.
options.explanation: This captures the core optimization insight: the moat isn't just about fine-tuning in isolation—it's about data accumulation over time and at scale. A funded competitor doesn't just copy the approach; they create a flywheel (customer base → proprietary data → better model → more customers) that Carlos can no longer outrun. The answer names the specific mechanism by which waiting destroys his advantage: he loses the race to scale and data richness. This is the right mental model for why timing matters in moat-building.

options.option_text: He loses the first-mover advantage because competitors can now build on legal data they accumulate faster than he can. His delay means he's playing catch-up on both capability and customer base.
options.explanation: This identifies the right problem (competitive data accumulation) and recognizes that speed matters, but it frames the issue in terms of 'catch-up' rather than structural disadvantage. It misses the key insight that a well-funded competitor doesn't just catch up—they overtake by converting capital into scale, which converts into data, which compounds into an unreachable advantage. The answer is directionally correct but doesn't articulate the second-order effect that makes the situation genuinely fatal.

options.option_text: A competitor launches and takes some of his customers. Carlos has to work harder to retain the three firms he has and will lose time he could have spent on the moat.
options.explanation: This addresses only the visible symptom: customer churn and reactive work. It doesn't engage with the optimization criterion at all—which is about whether the moat survives as a defensible advantage. The answer treats this as a normal competitive pressure rather than a structural problem. It misses that the real issue isn't losing customers this quarter; it's that a funded competitor will build an unreachable moat faster than Carlos can, regardless of churn.

options.option_text: His competitive advantage actually stays intact because he already has paying customers and proprietary data from three firms. A late entrant can't easily replicate that relationship trust and institutional lock-in.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable—customer relationships and lock-in matter—but it misses the core dynamic. Three customers generating 24K MRR is not a moat; it's a foothold. A funded competitor with capital can: (1) hire experienced legal talent, (2) acquire larger customers faster, (3) accumulate more diverse legal data, (4) iterate more aggressively. Customer relationships without a defensible technology or data advantage will erode. The answer conflates having *some* customers with having a *defensible* advantage, which is the exact misconception that could trap Carlos into neither building a real moat nor raising capital.

questions.question_text: Is the bottleneck his ability to serve customers, or his ability to train the model faster than competitors?

options.option_text: The bottleneck is model training speed. If competitors can replicate his core AI capability in six months with standard fine-tuning, Carlos's only defensible advantage is proprietary data + a trained model that works better. Customer features are commoditizable; a faster-trained moat is not. Revenue without moat is a ticking clock.
options.explanation: This identifies the true optimization criterion: defensibility over immediate revenue. It recognizes that in a commoditizing market, the bottleneck is not demand (three customers are asking for features) but rather competitive insulation. It correctly frames the sacrifice: short-term revenue growth for long-term business viability. This is the core reasoning move of the optimize step—naming what actually constrains survival.

options.option_text: Both are bottlenecks, but model training is more urgent because customers will churn if competitors ship a 'good enough' model faster than Carlos ships features. He needs to lock in a technical lead before it's too late.
options.explanation: Correctly identifies model training as the critical bottleneck and acknowledges the time pressure. However, it frames the problem as a race rather than a choice—it doesn't fully articulate the actual sacrifice (which is near-term revenue and fundraising runway). It hints at the right decision but doesn't cleanly separate the two competing constraints or name the optimization criterion explicitly.

options.option_text: The bottleneck is customer satisfaction. If Carlos doesn't ship the features his three customers are asking for, they'll leave. Losing existing customers is worse than losing future market share to competitors.
options.explanation: This focuses on the immediate, visible pressure (customer churn risk) but misses the structural problem. It treats the three customers as the primary constraint when the real constraint is competitive insulation in a fast-commoditizing space. A junior PM sees 'customers are unhappy' and optimizes for customer features; the optimize step requires naming why that optimization may kill the business anyway.

options.option_text: The bottleneck is fundraising. Carlos should ship customer features to hit $30K MRR, raise funding immediately, and then hire a team to build the moat in parallel. This solves both problems at once.
options.explanation: This sounds pragmatic and growth-focused, but it contradicts the scenario's constraint: Carlos has eight weeks of cash and competitors will have a working replica within six months. Raising money solves the runway problem but not the timing problem—by the time he closes a round and hires, his moat has already eroded. More fundamentally, this avoids the optimization choice rather than making it: it assumes he can do both in parallel when the scenario explicitly states both paths require his full focus.

win.step_nudge: Carlos should ship customer features now and use the revenue to fund moat-building at scale.

questions.question_text: What specific customer request should Carlos prioritize first to prove that the business model works?

options.option_text: Prioritize the customer request that reveals whether firms will pay for incremental improvement over their current workflow, not the one that sounds most impressive. Build the smallest version of it in two weeks, measure adoption and willingness to expand the contract, then use that signal to decide whether to invest in the moat or double down on features.
options.explanation: This answers the actual decision criterion: proving the business model works means proving customers will keep paying for iterative value, not just that they have pain. It names a testable hypothesis (adoption + contract expansion as proxy for model viability), sets a time constraint (two weeks) that preserves runway for the moat decision, and creates a decision rule (the signal informs whether to pursue depth or breadth). This is the 'win' move—make a specific, falsifiable call that de-risks the fundamental uncertainty.

options.option_text: Build the feature that multiple customers requested, since consensus demand reduces the risk that you're solving for one firm's idiosyncrasy. Ship it quickly, track usage, and use the traction to raise money faster.
options.explanation: Right instinct (pick something customers want, ship fast, measure signal), but misses the key risk: shipping fast and raising money doesn't *prove* the business model works if you haven't measured retention, expansion, or unit economics. It optimizes for fundraising narrative rather than for the actual decision (moat vs. features). Missing the threshold and decision rule—this lands in 'we have traction' rather than 'we know which path survives.'

options.option_text: Ask each of the three customers which feature they want most, then build that one first because it has explicit demand.
options.explanation: Addresses the visible symptom (customers want something), but doesn't address the strategic question. Picking the highest-voted feature doesn't answer whether shipping features *generally* is the right path versus building the moat. It treats the question as 'which feature' rather than 'should we feature-chase or moat-build,' and conflates customer loudness with business signal.

options.option_text: Prioritize whichever customer request most directly improves the core AI capability, since that's the only way to build a defensible moat in six months. Ignore requests that feel incremental and focus on the 'real' strategic work.
options.explanation: Sounds strategic (focus on the moat), but actually abandons the decision framework. Carlos's constraint is that he has eight weeks of cash and *both* paths feel correct—he can't pick moat work under the guise of 'customer-driven moat building.' This conflates two different questions and kills the business by investing in depth (moat) without proving anyone will pay for breadth (features). The whole point is to gather a signal that breaks the tie.

questions.question_text: How should he measure whether the two-month bet on customer features was the right call?

options.option_text: Track whether new customer features reduced churn and extended runway more than the moat investment would have. Specifically: if the three existing customers stay and add $8–15K MRR in new feature spending, AND that extension buys him time to raise on traction before capital runs out, the bet was defensible. The test is binary: did feature velocity keep customers long enough to reach a funding inflection point?
options.explanation: This captures the core reasoning move: the decision was made under a scarcity constraint (8 weeks cash). Measurement must answer whether the tactical choice actually bought time and proof-of-value better than the moat choice would have. It names the specific threshold (funding inflection), the timeline (before cash runs out), and the falsifiable metric (customer retention + MRR growth). It shows that 'the right call' isn't about moat vs. traction in abstract—it's about which path extends runway and gets to proof first under the actual constraint.

options.option_text: Measure whether feature delivery speed increased customer satisfaction scores and monthly retention by at least 20% compared to the previous quarter. If customers are happier and staying longer, the feature bet worked.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that customer retention and satisfaction are the right outcomes to track. However, it misses the strategic constraint: the decision was made because Carlos had 8 weeks of cash. A 20% retention bump is directionally right, but it doesn't answer the true win condition—whether that bump was *enough* to extend runway past the funding gate. It measures the feature work in isolation, not whether it achieved its strategic purpose under the time/capital constraint.

options.option_text: Measure feature adoption rates: how many of the three customers are using the new features within the first month of launch. High adoption means customers value the work.
options.explanation: This addresses the visible outcome (adoption) but confuses feature-building activity with strategic success. A customer can adopt a feature and still churn. The real question isn't whether the feature was built well—it's whether feature work was the right bet for *survival*. Adoption is a leading indicator, not the measure of whether the two-month gamble paid off.

options.option_text: Compare the moat-building effort to the feature work by measuring model accuracy improvements from fine-tuning vs. time-to-feature-ship. Whichever metric is higher proves the path he chose was better.
options.explanation: This optimizes for the wrong thing. It treats the decision as a technical comparison (model accuracy vs. feature velocity) when the actual constraint is business survival (cash runway). You can't measure a strategic trade-off by comparing the outputs of the two paths—you have to measure whether the chosen path met the survival threshold. This answer ignores the context entirely: the bet was made because Carlos needed proof + runway, not because feature velocity or model accuracy were the decision criteria.

questions.question_text: If competitors launch a comparable product within six months, what will actually protect Carlos' customers from switching?

options.option_text: The depth of integration into their existing workflows and data. If Carlos' product becomes the system of record—handling case intake, research, precedent tracking, and draft versioning—the switching cost is the effort to migrate years of institutional knowledge and custom configurations, not just the AI capability itself.
options.explanation: This captures the core win move: a defensible moat isn't the AI itself (easily copied), it's the lock-in created by depth of integration and data accumulation. Competitors with comparable AI still face high switching friction because they inherit empty databases and must re-integrate workflows. This is falsifiable—Carlos can test whether customers actually depend on historical data and workflow integration, not just the core algorithm.

options.option_text: The quality of Luma's coaching and the relationships Carlos builds with each firm's teams. If the product is supported by personalized guidance on how to use it effectively, switching means losing that institutional knowledge transfer, even if the competing AI is technically equivalent.
options.explanation: This identifies a real switching cost—service and relationships—but it's a secondary moat, not structural. Competitors can hire legal consultants and build their own support motion. This matters tactically (buy time, increase revenue) but doesn't address whether the business is fundamentally defensible. It's the right direction (beyond just the algorithm) but stops short of the structural lock-in that actually works.

options.option_text: The speed and accuracy of Carlos' AI compared to the competitors' models. If his model is measurably better at identifying relevant precedent, customers won't switch because the output quality is superior.
options.explanation: This addresses the visible symptom—the core capability—but misses the scenario's central insight. The prompt explicitly states competitors can copy the approach within six months with compute and legal data. Quality leads are temporary. This reasoning assumes the moat is the algorithm itself, which is precisely what Carlos can't defend. It's a junior answer that doesn't grapple with commoditization.

options.option_text: Carlos' brand reputation and track record of shipping new features faster than competitors. If customers perceive him as more innovative and responsive, they'll stay loyal even if the core AI becomes commodity.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable—brand and execution velocity matter—but it optimizes for the wrong thing. It assumes the customer relationship is personality-driven, which is weak for a six-person startup competing against well-funded teams that can hire top talent and ship faster. More critically, it sidesteps the core trade-off: Carlos doesn't have time to build brand moat *and* revenue moat. This reasoning would justify the revenue path but doesn't defend why customers actually stay once competitors arrive with comparable products and deeper pockets.

title: The Silent Bug and the Bigger Drop
scenario_context: Priya is a developer at Lens, a consumer photo-sharing app. The PM flagged a bug where the 'share to story' button sometimes fails silently. Priya quickly diagnosed and fixed a race condition in the upload queue that has been present for three weeks.
scenario_trigger: While investigating, Priya discovered that story shares have actually been declining for seven weeks—two weeks before the bug appeared. The PM's data shows a 40% drop, meaning something else caused the initial decline.
scenario_question: Should Priya ship the bug fix she's ready to deploy, or should she investigate the underlying cause of the earlier share decline first?
engineer_standout: Look at the dates. The drop started before the bug existed. What does that tell you about cause and effect?

frame.step_nudge: Define what problem you're actually solving. Fixing the reported bug and solving the real problem are not the same thing.

questions.question_text: The bug is real and you fixed it. But look at the timeline: what does the gap between the drop (week 1) and the bug (week 3) tell you about what's actually happening?

options.option_text: There are two separate problems: a recent technical bug and an earlier behavioral or product change. The gap tells you the 40% drop wasn't caused by button failures—users were already losing interest in story sharing before the bug even existed. Fixing the bug alone won't recover that 40%.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core frame move: distinguishing symptom from root cause. The responder correctly identifies that the timeline reveals two independent issues—the bug is a symptom you can fix, but the original 40% drop points to a separate root cause (product change, UX friction, feature deprecation, competitor shift, etc.). Fixing the bug is necessary but won't address what actually made story sharing less appealing. This is the 'why-before-how' reasoning at its essence.

options.option_text: The bug isn't the main problem. Something changed in how users perceive or use story sharing around week 1. The silent failure bug just made an existing problem worse when it appeared three weeks later.
options.explanation: This answer gets the directional insight right—something else happened first, and the bug is a secondary issue. But it's incomplete because it doesn't fully articulate *why* the gap matters strategically. It identifies that the bug 'made an existing problem worse' but doesn't explicitly say that fixing the bug won't recover the original 40% drop, and it doesn't push the responder to investigate what that 'something changed' actually was. It's a good intuition but lacks the full diagnostic clarity.

options.option_text: The bug was only one factor in the overall decline. We should ship the fix because it will at least stop new users from leaving, even if it doesn't fix the past two months.
options.explanation: This addresses the visible symptom (declining story shares) without uncovering the root cause. The responder acknowledges the bug is 'only one factor' but doesn't investigate *what the other factor is*. It settles on the expedient action (ship the fix) without the diagnostic work that frame requires. This is a junior answer: technically not wrong, but it avoids the harder thinking needed to scope the real problem.

options.option_text: The bug caused the initial 40% drop, but it took three weeks for the full impact to show up in the data. You should ship the fix immediately and expect story shares to recover to pre-drop levels.
options.explanation: This sounds plausible on the surface—bugs can have delayed effects, data can lag—but it contradicts the facts. Priya's data explicitly shows the drop started seven weeks ago and the bug appeared three weeks ago. Choosing this option misreads the timeline and assumes that fixing the bug will recover the 40%, which sets Priya up to ship, measure no improvement, and have to re-diagnose under time pressure. It optimizes for closure rather than truth.

questions.question_text: Priya could fix the button and declare victory. What assumption would she be making about why users stopped sharing stories?

options.option_text: That the 40% drop in story shares is entirely caused by the silent bug—that users stopped sharing because the button was broken, not because the feature or experience became less appealing.
options.explanation: This is the core framing insight. Priya is conflating two distinct problems: a technical failure (the bug, 3 weeks old) and a behavioral cliff (the drop, 7 weeks ago). By fixing only the button and assuming victory, she'd be assuming the root cause of the 40% decline is the bug—but the timeline proves otherwise. The real problem is something that happened 7 weeks ago that made story sharing less appealing to users. This demonstrates the frame step: distinguishing symptom (broken button) from root cause (whatever made users stop wanting to share).

options.option_text: That the bug is the primary driver of the drop, even though users may have started avoiding story shares for other reasons before the button broke.
options.explanation: This acknowledges that other reasons exist ('other reasons before the button broke'), but still treats the bug as the 'primary driver.' It's closer to frame thinking than pure symptom-focus, but it still misses the clean separation: the bug can't be the primary cause of a drop that predates it by 4 weeks. This answer conflates the problems rather than clearly distinguishing them.

options.option_text: That the silent bug is preventing users from sharing stories, which is why the button needs to be fixed.
options.explanation: This focuses only on the visible symptom—the broken button—and its immediate consequence. It doesn't engage with the timeline mismatch or the 40% drop at all. It's the instinct Priya has ('ship the fix and close the ticket'), but it ignores the deeper signal her gut is picking up. This is a junior answer that stops at the problem you can immediately fix.

options.option_text: That fixing the button will automatically restore the 40% of users who stopped sharing, because once the technical barrier is removed, their motivation to share will return unchanged.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable on the surface—of course you want to fix bugs—but it contains a hidden assumption that contradicts the evidence. It assumes user motivation is static and was only blocked by a technical barrier. But the 7-week timeline tells us motivation itself changed. Users didn't stop sharing because the button broke; they stopped wanting to share, and then the button broke 4 weeks later. Fixing the mechanism won't restore desire that was already lost. This optimizes for shipping over understanding.

questions.question_text: If you ship the fix without understanding the seven-week drop, what risk are you taking on?

options.option_text: You're treating a symptom (the button failure) as the root cause, so you'll ship a fix that solves the wrong problem. The 40% drop suggests users stopped wanting to share stories weeks before the button broke. Fixing the button won't bring them back if the underlying reason they stopped sharing is still there.
options.explanation: This captures the core frame reasoning: distinguishing symptom from root cause. It recognizes that the button bug is a downstream manifestation, not the driver of the original crater. It shows why-before-how thinking—you must understand *why* usage dropped before you can fix it. Shipping the button fix creates a false sense of progress while the real problem festers.

options.option_text: You'll ship a fix that technically works, but you won't know if it actually moves the needle on the 40% drop. Users might return once the button is fixed, or the drop might continue because something else is discouraging story shares. You'll have solved the bug but not validated whether it matters.
options.explanation: This identifies the right concern—that fixing the button might not address the real driver of the drop—but frames it as a validation problem rather than a root-cause problem. It's missing the deeper insight that you need to *diagnose* what caused the original crater *before* shipping, not validate afterward. Close, but stops short of the why-before-how move.

options.option_text: You'll get bug-fix credit but miss an easy win. Once the button is fixed, story shares will probably bounce back to normal levels, so the 40% drop will seem less urgent. But you should still fix it because users hate broken buttons.
options.explanation: This treats the symptom (broken button) as the whole story and assumes fixing it solves the problem. It doesn't recognize the temporal disconnect: the drop started seven weeks ago, the bug was there three weeks. It conflates 'broken button is bad' (true) with 'this broken button caused the 40% drop' (false). Junior reasoning.

options.option_text: Shipping the fix is the right call because you've identified and solved the bug quickly. The 40% drop is a separate business metric that the PM should investigate independently. Your job is to fix bugs; their job is to figure out why users aren't sharing. Getting the button working removes one blocker, which is always progress.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable (division of labor) but optimizes for the wrong goal. It treats the problem as a set of isolated tickets rather than an interconnected system. It misses the insight that Priya *has discovered* that the drop started before the bug—she has more signal than the PM does. Shipping blindly while the real driver goes undiagnosed is a missed opportunity to be strategic. This is the trap the instinct falls into.

list.step_nudge: Map the full space. There are multiple things that could cause story shares to crater—not just the button.

questions.question_text: List all the things that happened between week 1 (the drop) and week 3 (the bug appeared). What data points does Priya have access to?

options.option_text: Priya maps the seven-week timeline backward from the drop date. She checks: (1) feature releases or UI changes in the story flow, (2) algorithm changes affecting story visibility or engagement, (3) competitive actions (TikTok, Reels) that shifted user behavior, (4) infrastructure changes that may have degraded performance silently, (5) cohort-level data (new users vs. power users) to see if the drop is uniform or concentrated, (6) post-share conversion metrics (saves, shares, replies to stories) to understand if the problem is reaching the feature or converting after use. She cross-references timestamps in product logs, deployment records, and analytics dashboards.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core 'list' reasoning move: generating structurally distinct hypotheses (feature, algorithm, external, infra, cohort, conversion) and specifying the actual data artifacts Priya can access to test each one. It expands the stakeholder/factor map beyond 'the bug' and shows second-order thinking (who is affected, in what way, measurably).

options.option_text: Priya looks at feature releases and UI changes in the story flow over the past seven weeks. She also checks if there were any performance regressions or slow loading times that might have discouraged users. She reviews the product roadmap to see what shipped and talks to the PM about what was prioritized.
options.explanation: This captures the right direction—looking at product changes and performance—but stops short of the full structural map. It misses cohort-level analysis, competitive context, and concrete conversion metrics. It's grounded in product-level thinking but lacks the breadth to truly expand the stakeholder/cause map.

options.option_text: Priya looks at the bug logs from the past seven weeks to see if there were other upload-related issues that might have been silently failing before week 3. She checks error rates and retry counts to understand the scope of the silent failure.
options.explanation: This focuses on the symptom (the bug) rather than the root cause of the drop. The question asks what happened *before* the bug appeared—looking only at bug logs misses the upstream events that made story sharing less appealing. This is solving for the visible problem, not the underlying cause.

options.option_text: Priya runs a full A/B test on the story share button UI (color, size, placement) to see which version users prefer most. She also surveys users about their story-sharing habits to understand what would make them share more. This will tell her what she needs to fix to recover the 40% drop.
options.explanation: This sounds like good product thinking (test and iterate, listen to users) but it optimizes for the wrong thing. Priya already has a specific, time-bound drop that predates the bug by four weeks. Running a prospective experiment on UI variations doesn't explain *what changed* in the past. She needs to diagnose the actual cause first, not hypothesize about user preferences.

questions.question_text: Besides 'button broken,' what are three other reasons someone might stop sharing stories to their followers?

options.option_text: The feature itself became less useful because Stories are now oversaturated—everyone's posting them, the audience has grown tired, so the perceived impact of sharing dropped, reducing motivation to use it even when it works.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core list-phase reasoning: identifying a structural change in user motivation independent of product mechanics. It accounts for second-order effects (feature saturation → audience fatigue → reduced perceived value) and reframes the problem away from the button's availability to the user's changing incentive to share. This is the kind of insight that prevents fixing the wrong thing.

options.option_text: The Stories feature was deprioritized in the UI—moved lower in the menu or made less discoverable—making it harder to find even though it still functioned.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies a structural change (not the button itself) that would suppress usage. It's a legitimate hypothesis about why engagement fell. However, it's primarily a product-mechanics problem (discoverability), not a motivation or market-saturation problem. It's the kind of thing that would explain some drop but wouldn't account for a 40% sustained decline over 7 weeks—it assumes a one-time UI change, not a compounding user-behavior shift.

options.option_text: The share button is broken so users clicked it, it failed, and they stopped trying to use that feature.
options.explanation: This is the symptom Priya already identified and fixed. It explains why the button stopped working recently, but the problem statement explicitly says the drop started 7 weeks ago and the bug only appeared 3 weeks ago. This answer treats the bug as the root cause when it's only a contributor to the tail end of a larger decline—it's circular reasoning, not root-cause thinking.

options.option_text: A competitor (like TikTok or BeReal) launched a Stories-like feature that was better executed, so users migrated their sharing behavior elsewhere entirely.
options.explanation: This sounds like a reasonable market hypothesis but it contradicts the frame of the challenge. Priya is looking for reasons *within her control* as a product engineer—things she can investigate and act on using internal data (logs, user behavior, feature flags, analytics). External competitive migration is a macro-market question, not a 'what did we change' question. It also wrongly assumes causation from outside the company rather than exploring what Lens did differently 7 weeks ago.

questions.question_text: Who would Priya need to talk to—beyond logs and metrics—to understand the real shift?

options.option_text: Users who stopped sharing stories to see if their reasons shifted (privacy concerns, social anxiety, changed use case) and power users still sharing to understand what they do differently
options.explanation: This is the core list reasoning move: expanding the stakeholder map structurally. It distinguishes two distinct user segments (churned vs. retained) with opposite signals—one shows *why* sharing became less appealing, the other shows what mitigates that reason. This reveals second-order effects (the bug didn't cause the drop; something about the feature or context did). Interviewing both groups reveals the real shift, not just correlation.

options.option_text: Customer support to see if there's a pattern in complaints or feedback about story sharing before the bug was introduced
options.explanation: Right direction—looking for qualitative signals beyond metrics. Support conversations *can* surface why users stopped sharing. But this approach is passive (waiting for complaints) and limited to users who bothered to complain. It misses the power-user segment (why some *kept* sharing) and doesn't actively probe the shift. Incomplete without also talking to churned users directly or behavioral researchers.

options.option_text: The QA team to understand if there were other unreported bugs or edge cases in the share flow that could explain the drop
options.explanation: This stays in the symptom layer. It assumes the drop was caused by *another bug* rather than a product or user motivation shift. Priya already found one bug; searching for hidden bugs is technical troubleshooting, not understanding the *real* problem. It doesn't expand the stakeholder map or explore second-order effects—it's just digging deeper into the logs, which Priya already did.

options.option_text: Analytics to correlate the 40% drop with specific demographic cohorts so we can release the fix to high-value users first and measure recovery
options.explanation: This sounds data-driven and pragmatic, but it optimizes for *shipping faster* rather than *understanding the real shift*. Segmenting by cohort tells you *who* stopped sharing, not *why*. And it skips the critical discovery step—understanding if the drop was caused by the bug at all. If the real driver was a feature design or UX issue, shipping the bug fix alone won't recover the 40%, and Priya will have missed the real problem. This contradicts the reasoning move of understanding before acting.

optimize.step_nudge: Compare your options against what matters. What are you optimizing for—a quick win, or user behavior?

questions.question_text: If Priya ships the fix now, what does she learn in the next two weeks? If she digs into the drop first, what does she learn?

options.option_text: If she ships the fix now: she learns whether the bug was blocking 40% of users from succeeding, or whether only a small fraction hit the race condition while most stopped for other reasons. If she digs first: she learns what changed seven weeks ago that made story sharing less appealing—UI friction, feed algorithm change, notification settings, competitive pressure—and can fix the root cause instead of just restoring a broken feature.
options.explanation: This answer captures the core optimize reasoning: naming the decision criterion (whether the bug is the primary lever or just surface-level), articulating what each path teaches about the real problem, and showing that the sacrifice isn't bug-vs-root-cause but rather learning scope. Shipping the fix reveals data; investigating first prevents misallocation. It demonstrates why-before-how.

options.option_text: If she ships the fix now: she can measure the lift from the bug fix and estimate how much of the 40% drop it was responsible for. If she digs into the drop first: she can identify other user friction points that might be easier wins than fixing the race condition.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies that shipping gives measurement data and that digging reveals other friction points. It touches the right concept—distinguishing the bug's actual impact from the larger trend—but doesn't go deep enough on the sacrifice: it doesn't fully articulate what Priya won't know if she ships first (whether she's solving a real blocker or chasing a symptom while the real problem compounds).

options.option_text: If she ships the fix now: story shares will go back up and the ticket closes. If she digs first: she might find something, but the bug will still exist and users will keep hitting it.
options.explanation: This answer only sees the visible symptom (the bug exists, fixing it makes the feature work again). It misses the optimization frame entirely—the real question isn't bug-vs-investigation, it's about which path teaches you whether you're solving the right problem. It treats the two as mutually exclusive rather than sequencing decisions by what you need to learn.

options.option_text: If she ships the fix now: she can get credit for solving a bug quickly and move to the next ticket, which is efficient use of sprint time. If she digs first: she might waste time investigating when the fix is already ready, and the 40% drop will only get worse while she deliberates.
options.explanation: This sounds pragmatic on the surface (speed, efficiency, shipping value) but actually optimizes for the wrong thing. It assumes the bug fix addresses the core problem and frames investigation as delay. In fact, Priya's gut is telling her the opposite: shipping the fix without understanding the root cause will likely give false confidence that the problem is solved, when 40% of users still have no reason to share stories. It confuses velocity with direction.

questions.question_text: The PM is expecting a closed ticket this sprint. What are the consequences of each choice (ship vs. dig) on sprint velocity and on product clarity?

options.option_text: Shipping the fix closes velocity (one ticket resolved) but creates a false clarity problem: the PM will see a merged PR and assume the 40% drop is fixed, when the real crater happened earlier and remains undiagnosed. This invites a second, harder discovery phase in two weeks when numbers don't recover. Digging first trades this sprint's velocity for product clarity—you identify the true cause, document it, and ship both fixes together, giving the PM an accurate read on impact.
options.explanation: This answer nails the optimize frame: it names the real sacrifice (sprint velocity vs. product clarity), shows second-order consequences (false confidence leading to rework), and articulates why clarity now is worth the short-term velocity hit. It demonstrates the core reasoning move—distinguishing the symptom (bug) from the cause (the earlier drop) and showing how each choice affects decision-making downstream.

options.option_text: Shipping the fix keeps sprint velocity intact and shows progress to the PM, but you'll discover the root cause of the 40% drop later when it becomes urgent (maybe in an incident or when a stakeholder asks). Digging now means pausing the fix, which adds risk if users keep hitting the button bug. The real trade is between immediate credibility (shipping) and preventing future surprises (investigating).
options.explanation: This answer identifies a legitimate trade-off (credibility vs. prevention) and shows some second-order thinking about timing and risk. However, it frames the problem as a risk-management issue rather than a clarity/decision-making issue. It misses the deeper point: that shipping without understanding creates a false signal to the PM about what fixed the problem, which is a *clarity* failure, not just a risk issue.

options.option_text: Shipping the fix solves the immediate problem—users will stop experiencing the failed share button. The 40% drop is a separate issue that can be investigated later without blocking sprint progress. Velocity is maximized by closing the ticket now and opening a new one for the broader drop.
options.explanation: This answer treats the bug and the drop as completely independent issues and optimizes only for velocity (closing tickets). It ignores the deeper insight: that the two are causally linked in diagnosis, and that shipping the fix alone creates a false signal about what the real problem is. It's a junior framing that solves the visible symptom without considering product clarity or the PM's mental model.

options.option_text: Digging into the original drop will likely uncover multiple root causes that require cross-team coordination and planning. This guarantees the sprint will miss velocity targets and delay the bug fix further, frustrating the PM. Ship the fix now to restore user trust in the button, then commit to a full postmortem after sprint closes. This separates urgent user-facing work from longer-term analysis.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable (ship fast, analyze later) but it optimizes for the wrong thing and contradicts the core reasoning move. It assumes digging will be complex and lengthy, when the real insight is that Priya already has data—she can diagnose quickly. More critically, it perpetuates the clarity problem: the PM will ship the fix, numbers won't recover, and only *then* will the team realize the bug wasn't the cause. The answer conflates urgency (ship the button fix) with understanding (diagnose the drop), when the optimize frame demands naming what you're sacrificing and why clarity matters here.

questions.question_text: What would success look like for each path? Fewer crashes vs. restored user behavior are very different goals.

options.option_text: Success means identifying what changed seven weeks ago that made users less interested in sharing to stories—then measuring whether fixing *that* restores the 40% drop. The silent bug fix is necessary but won't solve the original problem. The real metric is user behavior (share volume, engagement), not error rates.
options.explanation: This captures the core optimize move: naming the actual success criterion (restored user behavior, not crash fixes) and distinguishing it from the symptom fix. It shows why-before-how thinking—understanding that two separate problems exist with different root causes. The answer refuses to optimize for the visible metric (bug) when the real constraint is the invisible one (user motivation). This is the right mental model for the optimize frame.

options.option_text: Success means shipping the bug fix to stop the silent failures, then running an experiment to isolate what caused the earlier drop. We'll measure the impact of each fix separately and see which one moves the needle.
options.explanation: This shows sound reasoning—separating the two problems and testing them independently. The answer correctly identifies that we need to understand both issues. But it doesn't articulate the sacrifice or the optimization criterion clearly. It defers the real decision instead of making it explicit: what are we optimizing for first? What's the priority trade-off? Incomplete on the 'articulating the sacrifice' dimension of optimize.

options.option_text: Success means fixing the silent bug so users can share to stories again without the button failing. Once that's shipped, story share volume should return to normal, and we can close out the bug ticket.
options.explanation: This treats the visible symptom (the button failing) as the whole problem. It assumes that fixing the crash will restore user behavior—which contradicts the data. The drop started before the bug appeared. This answer collapses two distinct problems into one and misses the frame entirely. It's the 'instinct' Priya had, not the 'gut' answer that recognizes something deeper is wrong.

options.option_text: Success means measuring the reduction in silent failures (from crash logs) and ensuring we hit zero errors before investigating the older drop. We should fully stabilize the current bug before looking backward at what happened weeks ago.
options.explanation: This sounds disciplined (fix one thing at a time), but it inverts the optimization criterion. It prioritizes metric hygiene (crash logs) over user behavior (engagement). It also delays understanding the root cause of 40% lost revenue to chase perfect error metrics on a secondary problem. This is the trap of optimizing for engineering cleanliness instead of product impact—solving for the visible problem while the real business problem festers.

win.step_nudge: Make a call. You can't do both in one sprint. What do you recommend and why?

questions.question_text: Should Priya ship the button fix now or hold it while she investigates the seven-week drop? State your recommendation as a single sentence.

options.option_text: Hold the fix and spend 1-2 days investigating the seven-week drop before shipping anything, because fixing a symptom while the root cause still crushes engagement is a false win that masks the bigger problem.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the core 'win' move: making a specific, defensible, falsifiable call. It names the sacrifice (delay the small fix), states the criterion (root cause matters more than symptom relief), and implies a test (investigate first, then decide). It recognizes that a 40% drop over seven weeks is the real problem; the three-week-old bug is only a contributing factor. Shipping the fix without understanding the original crater wastes the opportunity to reverse the bigger loss.

options.option_text: Ship the fix immediately as a hotfix while investigating the drop in parallel, so users get relief sooner and the investigation doesn't block progress.
options.explanation: This partially applies the reasoning move: it acknowledges that the bug fix has value and doesn't want to waste time. However, it misses the critical insight that shipping the fix may *obscure* the root cause of the original drop. Once the button works again, engagement metrics become noisy, making it harder to isolate what caused the seven-week crater. It's not purely wrong (parallel work is feasible), but it sacrifices diagnostic clarity for velocity.

options.option_text: Ship the fix now because the bug is real and users are frustrated when the button fails silently, so fixing a broken feature is the right priority.
options.explanation: This addresses only the visible symptom (broken button) and ignores the underlying insight: the bug is not the root cause of the 40% drop. It's a junior's answer—focus on the defect in front of you without stepping back to ask why engagement collapsed weeks before the bug appeared. It optimizes for shipping a fix, not for understanding the real problem.

options.option_text: Ship the fix immediately because the bug is blocking 3% of story shares, and fixing it will recover most of the lost 40% and prove the investment is worth investigating further.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable on the surface (ship quick wins first), but it's factually wrong and contradicts the scenario: the bug has only existed for three weeks, yet the drop started seven weeks ago. Shipping this fix will *not* recover the 40%—it will only recover a small fraction of it. This reasoning leads to false confidence (the fix ships, engagement barely budges, Priya looks ineffective) and wastes the window to diagnose the real problem. It optimizes for shipping over understanding.

questions.question_text: What is the one piece of evidence that would tell Priya whether her recommendation was right?

options.option_text: Ship the bug fix, then measure story share attempt rate over the next two weeks. If it rises back toward pre-drop baseline after the fix, the bug was the root cause. If it stays flat, the original behavior change seven weeks ago is the real problem.
options.explanation: This captures the core win move: making a specific, falsifiable call with a clear test and threshold. It isolates the variables (bug fix vs. original behavior change) and proposes a measurable outcome that will definitively answer whether Priya's recommendation was correct. The two-week timeline is defensible given sprint cycles, and the baseline comparison is the right metric to evaluate against. It doesn't assume—it tests.

options.option_text: After shipping the fix, check if story share completion rate increases. If users are now successfully sharing once they tap the button, the bug was worth fixing. If completion rate stays the same, the issue is upstream.
options.explanation: This identifies the right testing approach (measure post-fix behavior) and understands that completion rate matters. However, it misses the critical threshold and timeline specifics. More importantly, it doesn't address the core tension: completion rate improving doesn't tell Priya whether the bug was the *root cause* of the seven-week drop. It conflates 'the fix works' with 'the fix solves the real problem.' A stronger version would name a specific recovery target and timeline.

options.option_text: Monitor error logs after the fix deploys. If the race condition errors disappear, the bug is fixed. If errors persist, there's a deeper technical issue to debug.
options.explanation: This focuses purely on the mechanical fix—does the code work?—not on answering Priya's actual decision question: was the bug the root cause of the seven-week drop? Error logs going silent proves the patch is correct but tells her nothing about user behavior or whether shipping now is the right call. It addresses the symptom (button failing) without touching the underlying strategic question (why did engagement crater before the button broke?).

options.option_text: Ask users directly via survey: 'Did the silent bug prevent you from sharing?' If enough users say yes, ship the fix immediately. If they say no, pause and investigate the earlier change.
options.explanation: This sounds reasonable on the surface (ask users!) but optimizes for the wrong thing. Users can't reliably diagnose the root cause of their own behavior—especially for something that happened weeks ago and was silent (no error message to recall). Survey answers are retrospective and biased; they won't cut through the ambiguity. Worse, this approach delays shipping a working fix while Priya waits for survey data, when a live A/B test or metric comparison would give faster, more reliable signals. It's plausible but introduces false confidence and delays action.

questions.question_text: If the original drop turns out to be unrelated to the button (like a feature change or a competitor launch), how does that change what Priya does next?

options.option_text: Ship the bug fix immediately—it's objectively right and removes friction. But flag the 40% drop as a separate strategic investigation: brief the PM that two problems exist (technical + product), and propose a post-ship diagnostic: which cohorts stopped sharing? When exactly? What changed in the app or market seven weeks ago? Use the bug fix as a baseline to isolate the real cause.
options.explanation: This demonstrates the win-level reasoning: make a specific, falsifiable, defensible call (ship the fix—it's objectively correct) AND name the next test with a timeline. It separates the two problems cleanly rather than conflating them. It doesn't use the bug fix as an excuse to ignore the bigger drop; instead, it uses the fix as a clean baseline to measure what's actually driving the decline. The follow-up diagnostic is concrete (cohort analysis, timeline audit, change log review).

options.option_text: Hold the bug fix and investigate the root cause of the 40% drop first. Once you understand what really happened seven weeks ago, you'll know whether the button fix is even worth shipping or if users have already churned past the point where fixing it matters.
options.explanation: This captures the insight that the two problems are separate and that the bigger problem deserves attention. It also recognizes that the product shift might have made the button fix less relevant. But it lacks the specificity and defensibility of a win-level answer: it doesn't name a concrete test, threshold, or timeline. It also risks false causality—delaying a bug fix waiting for investigation data can itself be costly. A win answer would make a call and then test.

options.option_text: The original drop is a distraction. The silent bug is real and measurable—Priya fixed it, the button now works, users will share more. Close the ticket, celebrate the quick fix, and move on to the next sprint.
options.explanation: This is a junior answer focused only on the visible, solvable problem. It ignores the PM's data signal entirely and assumes the bug fix will restore the 40% drop—but the scenario explicitly states the decline predates the bug by four weeks. It optimizes for shipping velocity and technical closure, not for understanding or solving the actual product problem.

options.option_text: If the original drop is unrelated to the button, then the button bug must be causing a cascading secondary effect—maybe users who couldn't share once are now permanently discouraged from using the app. So fix the button, then run a re-engagement campaign to bring back the 40% who already left.
options.explanation: This sounds logical but contradicts the scenario's data. The drop started seven weeks ago; the bug is three weeks old. A cascading discouragement would create new decline after the bug appeared, not before. This answer confuses correlation with causation and invents a mechanism to justify working on the wrong problem. It also treats the symptom (engaged users) instead of diagnosing what actually happened seven weeks ago.

title: API Docs at Stripe: Time vs. Trust
scenario_context: Marcus is a staff engineer on Stripe's developer experience team. For six months, his team has manually written API reference docs by parsing code comments and crafting examples—a process that takes three weeks per release. The PM has introduced an AI writing tool that can auto-generate docs from code comments in just a few hours, potentially freeing up 18 weeks of work annually. However, Marcus knows that developer NPS at Stripe correlates tightly with doc quality, and the AI tool captures only about 80% of what humans produce—missing edge cases, warnings, and the nuanced details that senior engineers depend on.
scenario_trigger: The PM needs a decision by Friday on whether to adopt the AI tool. Marcus must articulate the actual trade-off being made, not just the time savings.
scenario_question: What's the real cost of choosing speed over depth in developer documentation, and how should Marcus frame this decision to the PM?
engineer_standout: Think about who reads these docs and when they matter most. Is quality equally important for every audience and every use case?

frame.step_nudge: Define what you're actually choosing between. It's not time vs. effort—it's time vs. a specific kind of trust.

questions.question_text: What is the relationship between doc quality and Stripe's developer retention or NPS? What evidence does Marcus have?

options.option_text: Doc quality directly shapes how quickly developers can integrate and succeed with Stripe's APIs. Poor docs create friction (trial-and-error, support tickets, abandoned projects), which erodes NPS and increases churn to competitors. Marcus has six months of data showing correlation between his team's detailed edge-case docs and lower support volume + higher developer satisfaction scores in quarterly surveys—the 80% solution would reintroduce the problems those improvements solved.
options.explanation: This answer nails the frame move: it articulates the causal chain (doc quality → developer success → retention/NPS) and grounds it in concrete, measurable evidence Marcus would actually have (support ticket trends, survey scores, correlation data). It also implicitly names what's at stake—not just speed, but losing institutional knowledge about what devs struggle with. This is the reasoning a staff engineer at Stripe would use.

options.option_text: Developer NPS correlates with doc quality, and Marcus's team has noticed that detailed explanations of edge cases and common mistakes reduce support inquiries. However, Marcus doesn't have quantified evidence linking specific doc improvements to NPS changes—he's working mostly from intuition and team feedback rather than rigorous metrics.
options.explanation: This captures the right intuition (doc quality → NPS) and identifies one concrete indicator (support volume), but stops short of the full picture. It acknowledges the evidence gap, which is honest, but a staff engineer would have had six months to build that case—and if he's genuinely concerned about this trade, he likely has more data than just team feedback. It's incomplete because it doesn't claim the evidence Marcus should have gathered.

options.option_text: Better docs mean developers are happier because they have fewer questions and can ship faster. The AI tool will make docs faster to produce, so developers will get updates quicker, which should keep NPS up even if the docs are slightly less detailed.
options.explanation: This focuses only on the visible symptom (speed) and makes an unexamined assumption (that faster updates outweigh quality loss). It misses the frame entirely—the real question isn't whether docs help, but whether *this specific 80% solution* meets Stripe's bar. It also confuses 'fewer questions' with 'developer success,' which isn't the same thing when the missing 20% are edge cases that cause real failures.

options.option_text: NPS is driven primarily by Stripe's pricing, payment processing reliability, and feature breadth—not doc quality. Documentation is table stakes; as long as basic examples and endpoint descriptions are present, developers won't churn. The real risk is opportunity cost: if the team doesn't use this AI tool, competitors with faster doc cycles will pull developers away.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic (focusing on competitive differentiation) but gets the diagnosis backwards. It treats docs as commodity ('table stakes') when the scenario explicitly states that Marcus has evidence that doc *quality* (not just presence) correlates with NPS. It also inverts the trade: it's not 'use AI or lose to faster competitors'—it's 'keep quality high or lose developer trust.' For an API platform, docs are a core differentiator, not a commodity. This answer would lead Marcus to make the wrong trade.

questions.question_text: The AI tool is '80% as good' at docs. But 80% of what, exactly? Is it 80% complete coverage or 80% quality on each doc?

options.option_text: 80% quality on each doc is the real risk. If the AI misses edge cases and warnings on every endpoint, developers hit unexpected bugs in production. With manual docs, we lose time but keep trust. With AI docs at 80% quality, we save time but hand developers a liability—they'll learn to distrust the docs and stop using them, which is worse than no docs at all.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that 'quality per doc' is the actual constraint. It surfaces the second-order effect: broken trust cascades—developers stop relying on docs, making the time savings worthless. Marcus understands that API docs aren't just reference material; they're a trust mechanism. At 80% quality, each doc becomes a landmine, not a helpful guide. This is the frame he needs to articulate to the PM.

options.option_text: It depends on which endpoints are incomplete. If the AI handles 80% of endpoints fully and misses 20% entirely, that's maybe recoverable—developers know to dig deeper for those. But if it's 80% shallow across all endpoints, there's no clear signal for what to trust and what to verify independently.
options.explanation: This shows solid reasoning about coverage vs. depth and recognizes that distributed shallow errors create confusion. However, it misses the deeper insight: even with perfect coverage, 80% quality on each doc erodes trust globally. The framing here is more tactical (which endpoints get hit) rather than strategic (how developers' relationship to documentation changes). It's a partial move toward the right frame.

options.option_text: 80% as good means the tool catches most of the common cases but might miss some advanced features. We could pair the AI tool with a light review process—maybe spot-check 20% of the docs—and ship faster than manual writing while staying mostly safe.
options.explanation: This treats the problem as a coverage/completeness issue solvable by light review. It misses the frame entirely: the question isn't whether 20% spot-checking is 'enough,' but what 80% quality *means* for developer trust and behavior. A junior answer that jumps to mitigation tactics without naming the real trade.

options.option_text: 80% as good is actually fine because Stripe's most sophisticated users—the ones who drive NPS—are using Stripe's source code and SDKs as reference anyway. For them, API docs are secondary. We should prioritize speed and let power users self-serve.
options.explanation: This sounds strategic but inverts Marcus's core insight. Stripe's NPS correlation *is* with doc quality—and the docs matter most for the *breadth* of developers, not just power users. Dismissing doc quality as 'secondary for sophisticated users' abandons the insight that got Marcus into the room: junior engineers rely on docs to avoid mistakes, and that reliability builds trust. This optimizes for the wrong stakeholder.

questions.question_text: Who benefits from AI-generated docs and who gets hurt? Is it the same across all developer skill levels?

options.option_text: Senior and mid-level engineers benefit from AI docs (faster discovery of basic patterns), but junior engineers and on-call engineers get hurt—they rely on edge-case warnings and defensive patterns that AI misses. The same doc set hurts different users differently depending on their risk tolerance and experience level.
options.explanation: This identifies the core tension: AI-generated docs create a stratified outcome where experienced engineers can interpolate missing context, but junior engineers and those debugging under pressure need the exact guardrails and gotchas that manual docs capture. It maps the trade specifically to skill level, showing Marcus understands that 'everyone gets 80%' is actually 'some people get hurt disproportionately.' This is the frame—the real problem isn't speed, it's distributional harm.

options.option_text: The tool benefits product teams and PMs (faster releases, less internal toil), while developers lose quality and depth. But quality impact varies—experienced engineers can work around shallow docs, while less experienced ones struggle more.
options.explanation: This correctly identifies that stakeholders split (internal org vs. external devs) and that skill level matters for impact. But it stops short of naming the specific downstream cost: increased support burden, production incidents from missed edge cases, or developer churn. It maps the outcome but doesn't fully trace the second-order effects or name who bears the actual business risk.

options.option_text: Junior developers benefit because they get docs faster. Senior developers don't benefit as much because they already know the patterns. So the AI tool helps the people who need help most.
options.explanation: This mistakes speed for utility and misses the quality-depth axis entirely. It assumes junior developers benefit from faster, shallower docs—the opposite of the real dynamic. It's a surface reading that confuses 'more docs' with 'better docs for learners,' ignoring that junior engineers specifically need the defensive patterns and warnings that AI misses.

options.option_text: AI-generated docs help everyone equally because they cover the same API surface. The real problem is just that Marcus's team doesn't want to automate their jobs. The trade is between job security and engineering velocity.
options.explanation: This frames the problem as organizational resistance rather than product risk, and assumes uniform benefit ('same API surface'). It's plausible—engineering teams do resist automation—but it completely misses the actual trade Marcus is making: between speed and developer safety/learning, and who gets harmed. It optimizes for the wrong thing (job preservation vs. product quality) and obscures the real distributional question.

list.step_nudge: Map the space of outcomes. There are more than two ways to solve the time problem.

questions.question_text: Besides 'use AI for everything' or 'keep writing by hand,' what are two other paths Stripe could take?

options.option_text: Use AI to draft docs from code comments, then route drafts through a tiered review: junior engineers flag what they'd find confusing, senior engineers add edge-case warnings and 'common mistakes' sections. Ship only after both tiers sign off.
options.explanation: This option identifies the actual trade: speed vs. trust. It doesn't reject the AI tool's efficiency gain (drafts in hours instead of weeks), but it protects the signal that drives NPS—the depth that comes from human judgment about what *breaks* and what users *misunderstand*. It introduces a two-stage filter that separates what AI can do (breadth, structure) from what humans must do (depth, wisdom). The tiering also multiplies judgment: junior engineers catch obvious gaps, seniors catch subtle ones. This directly addresses Marcus's insight that 80% isn't good enough without losing the time gain.

options.option_text: Segment endpoints by risk: auto-generate docs for straightforward, well-tested endpoints with few parameters. Keep the manual process for complex, high-risk, or frequently-misused endpoints that need depth.
options.explanation: This option makes a defensible trade: apply AI where the cost of missing edge cases is low, keep humans where the cost is high. It's a real middle path that saves time on the 'safe' 60% of endpoints and protects quality on the 'risky' 40%. However, it doesn't fully solve the problem—it still requires humans to *manually* write for the hard cases, leaving the time savings modest (maybe 30-40% reduction, not 80%). It's incomplete because it doesn't suggest how to *improve* the AI output or create human-AI feedback loops that could raise the floor for *all* docs over time.

options.option_text: Use the AI tool as-is but hire an additional technical writer to audit the AI docs after each release and flag anything that looks incomplete or wrong.
options.explanation: This option sees the quality gap ('80% is not good enough') and tries to patch it with labor. But it solves the symptom, not the root cause. You'd still ship incomplete docs to developers first, then apologize and fix them later—eroding the trust that NPS measures. You've also negated most of the time saving (add 1-2 weeks for audit + revision). This is reactive firefighting dressed up as a strategy.

options.option_text: Accept that developer NPS will drop slightly in the short term, use AI to free up the team for higher-impact work like building an interactive API explorer or sandbox environment that adds more value than perfect docs.
options.explanation: This sounds like strategic thinking—trade one metric for another, use freed capacity for bigger bets. But it misdiagnoses the constraint. The PM brought the AI tool *because* the team is overloaded writing docs by hand. Freeing them up doesn't solve the underlying problem: you still need the docs to be *good* to ship the product. An interactive explorer is nice-to-have, but if developers can't understand the API in the first place, the explorer doesn't rescue NPS. This option optimizes for the *wrong* trade: it sacrifices the thing that *currently* drives trust (doc quality) to buy time for something else, betting that 'something else' will more than make up for it. That's a high-risk, unvalidated bet.

questions.question_text: What if Stripe used AI to generate first drafts, then applied human review? What would that cost in time and what would it gain?

options.option_text: It would take roughly 1-2 weeks per release instead of 3 weeks (AI draft + review), freeing up 6-12 weeks per year. The gain is that you preserve the 80% accuracy floor from AI while human reviewers catch the edge cases and warnings that correlate with developer NPS. The trade is you're now bottlenecked on review throughput, not writing throughput—so the time savings plateau if your review capacity is limited.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the hybrid model as a shifting constraint problem, not just a linear time reduction. It quantifies both the time gain (specific weeks saved) and the NPS-preserving mechanism (catching what AI misses), then reveals the second-order effect: review becomes the new bottleneck. This is the core reasoning move for 'list'—expanding the stakeholder map and showing structural trade-offs, not just surface metrics.

options.option_text: It would save time compared to full manual writing, maybe 2-3 weeks per release, since humans still have to review every doc. The benefit is better quality than AI-only, the cost is it still requires senior engineer attention, so you haven't really solved the capacity problem.
options.explanation: This identifies the right structural insight—that review is still a constraint and senior engineers are still required—but it doesn't articulate what second-order effect that creates. It acknowledges the time-quality trade but stops short of naming what the bottleneck becomes or how review capacity would scale. Missing the 'what specifically gets constrained' precision.

options.option_text: It would be faster than manual writing and better quality than AI-only. The time savings would depend on how long reviews take, and the quality would be better because humans catch things AI misses.
options.explanation: This is surface-level tautology—it restates the visible benefit (faster + better) without analyzing structure. It doesn't quantify time savings, doesn't name which stakeholders are affected, doesn't reveal what new constraint emerges. Treats the hybrid model as obviously good without doing the work of 'list'—which is to expand the stakeholder map and show second-order effects.

options.option_text: It would take almost the same time as manual docs because human review of AI work is as slow as writing from scratch. So the hybrid approach is a false economy—just automate everything and accept the 20% accuracy loss, since shipping speed matters more to developers than edge-case warnings.
options.explanation: This contradicts the premise that developer NPS correlates with doc quality (stated in the scenario), and it makes a false equivalence: review of a draft is not the same time cost as writing from scratch. More critically, it optimizes for the wrong variable—shipping speed over the actual developer outcome that Stripe's NPS data shows matters. Sounds decisive but ignores the data that frames the actual trade.

questions.question_text: If Stripe cuts the doc scope (fewer examples, fewer edge cases) to free up time, what happens to developer experience?

options.option_text: Developers hit invisible walls: the happy path works, but common gotchas and integration patterns aren't documented. Senior engineers know to dig deeper; juniors get stuck, churn increases, and NPS drops because Stripe feels incomplete, not broken.
options.explanation: This captures the core trade Marcus is naming: cutting scope doesn't just save time, it shifts *who* the docs serve. Stripe's NPS is built on serving junior + mid-level developers with depth they can't get elsewhere. Removing that depth doesn't degrade docs uniformly—it creates a trust gap where developers feel abandoned when they hit edge cases. This shows second-order thinking about *user segmentation* and *trust erosion*, not just feature loss.

options.option_text: Developers will find workarounds or switch to competitors' docs, which are more complete. The team saves time but loses the trust advantage that made Stripe's docs differentiate.
options.explanation: This identifies the right consequence (trust loss, competitive risk) but misses the *structural mechanism*. It names the outcome (developers leave) without explaining *why* scope cuts matter more than time savings for this specific customer segment. A strong answer would explain that docs are a *reliability signal*—juniors need reassurance that edge cases are handled, not just that happy paths exist. This gets the direction right but lacks the depth analysis.

options.option_text: Developers will get frustrated when they run into issues the docs don't cover, so they'll give lower satisfaction scores to Stripe.
options.explanation: This describes the immediate symptom (frustration → lower NPS) but doesn't explain the *mechanism* or the *differentiation at stake*. It treats docs as a generic product feature ('cover more stuff = happier users') rather than recognizing that for developer platforms, docs are a *trust proxy*. Doesn't distinguish between 'docs are less convenient' and 'docs are unreliable', which is the real problem Marcus sees.

options.option_text: Cutting scope has minimal impact because 80% coverage is still good enough—most developers only need the basics, and Stripe can supplement with community forums and Stack Overflow answers for edge cases.
options.explanation: This sounds like pragmatic math (80% is 'good enough'), but it contradicts Marcus's actual insight: doc quality *correlates tightly with NPS*, meaning Stripe's developers don't treat Stripe's docs as optional. This option assumes developers are indifferent to doc depth and will happily fill gaps themselves—but senior engineers specifically rely on Stripe's depth, and juniors lose confidence when they can't trust the docs are complete. It optimizes for speed at the expense of the actual value driver (trust), which is exactly the wrong trade.

optimize.step_nudge: Name the criterion. What is Stripe optimizing for—and what are you willing to trade?

questions.question_text: Stripe's competitive advantage in the payment space is partly that developers trust the docs. How much time savings is worth a small dent in that trust?

options.option_text: It's not about the raw hours saved—it's about what you're trading away. If the AI tool captures 80% of the depth that builds developer trust, you're accepting a small but permanent erosion of your competitive moat in exchange for temporary team bandwidth. The real question is: what would you ship with those 18 freed weeks that's worth risking that trust? If the answer is 'nothing strategic,' you shouldn't do it.
options.explanation: This option names the actual optimization criterion: developer trust as a competitive advantage. It reframes the trade explicitly—not time vs. task, but time vs. moat. It articulates the sacrifice (permanent quality dent) and demands clarity on what fills the freed-up capacity. This is the core move for 'optimize': making the trade-off visible and conditional on what you do with the savings.

options.option_text: 20% of the details are what separate Stripe from competitors in the long term, so losing that is risky. But if you could use the freed time to build something that actually improves the developer experience—like interactive API explorers or better error messages—then the trade makes sense. You'd be swapping static doc depth for dynamic docs quality.
options.explanation: This option correctly identifies the core trade (depth vs. automation) and recognizes that the freed time matters only if reinvested wisely. However, it doesn't fully articulate the risk: it assumes the new product (explorers, error messages) would outweigh the trust erosion from thinner docs. It's moving in the right direction but hasn't named the guardrail—what metric would tell you the trade was a mistake?

options.option_text: 18 weeks a year is a lot of time. Your team could use that capacity to ship faster, which developers care about. The AI tool handles the routine docs, and your team reviews and adds the edge cases when they come up.
options.explanation: This option focuses only on the visible benefit (time savings) without acknowledging the core tension: developer trust as a competitive advantage. It assumes edge cases can be 'added when they come up,' but that's reactive, not proactive—and doesn't address the speed-to-discovery problem for new users. It's addressing the symptom, not the strategic trade.

options.option_text: The tool is 80% accurate, so you'd only lose 20% of quality. That's acceptable—most developers won't notice, and the ones who do are probably advanced enough to figure it out anyway. Use the 18 weeks to improve other parts of the developer experience that matter more than doc perfection.
options.explanation: This sounds logical on the surface but misses the core insight from the scenario: developer NPS correlates tightly with doc quality, and the 20% that's missing—edge cases, warnings, 'here's what junior engineers get wrong'—is disproportionately important for trust-building. It also assumes 'most developers won't notice,' which contradicts Stripe's competitive positioning. It optimizes for the wrong thing (process efficiency) instead of the thing that matters (trust).

questions.question_text: If the docs get 20% worse, how long does it take for that to show up in NPS or churn? What would Marcus see in the data?

options.option_text: It depends on the velocity of new integrations. If 20% of developers hit edge cases in the first 2-3 weeks after a release, support tickets spike immediately—Marcus would see it in weekly support metrics within 7-10 days. But NPS lag is longer: it typically takes 4-6 weeks for doc-induced friction to surface in quarterly NPS surveys. The real leading indicator is support volume and resolution time, not the NPS number itself.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the optimize move: naming the actual metric you can measure (support tickets as a leading indicator) versus the lagging metric (NPS). It shows that Marcus needs to instrument the right observable signal—support friction emerges within days, but NPS is a trailing measure. This is the core insight: you measure what changes first, not what the PM cares about most.

options.option_text: Marcus should expect to see increased support ticket volume and longer resolution times within 2-3 weeks. The NPS impact would follow 1-2 quarters later. But he should also track developer sentiment via community channels (forums, Discord, GitHub issues) as a real-time proxy for frustration, since those are more immediate than formal surveys.
options.explanation: This answer correctly identifies the lag between incident and NPS, and adds a useful monitoring layer (community sentiment). However, it doesn't articulate the key insight about causality: that Marcus needs to measure whether the 20% degradation *actually caused* the churn, not just assume correlation. It's missing the guardrail—how do you know the support spike is doc-related and not something else?

options.option_text: NPS would drop noticeably within one quarter because most developer satisfaction surveys run quarterly. Marcus should set up a pulse survey after each release to catch feedback faster, and compare doc-quality ratings before and after the AI rollout.
options.explanation: This focuses on the timing of the survey mechanism itself rather than the underlying causal lag. It treats NPS as a direct measure of doc quality without addressing the real question: how long between a developer encountering a bad doc and that experience degrading their perception of Stripe? The answer conflates 'when we measure NPS' with 'when the impact shows up in behavior.'
